section.title: Further Sayings of the Wise
section.sectionRationale: A short supplement follows the thirty sayings, explicitly introduced as 'also sayings of the wise,' 24:23, before the book moves into the Hezekiah collection in chapter 25. It presses on courtroom honesty and closes with a vivid, first-person parable of a lazy man's ruined field, learned by observation rather than command.
section.thesis: Honest judgment matters more than smooth partiality, and the ruin that overtakes a lazy man's field is a lesson meant to be watched, taken to heart, and heeded before poverty arrives like a robber.
section.frameNote: Two clusters: honest judgment versus partiality and flattery; the observed parable of the sluggard's ruined field and the poverty that follows a little more sleep.

verses.text: These also are sayings of the wise: To show partiality in judgment is not good.
Whoever tells the guilty, "You are innocent," peoples will curse him, and nations will denounce him;
but it will go well with those who convict the guilty, and rich blessing will come upon them.
An honest answer given is like a kiss on the lips.
Complete your outdoor work and prepare your field; after that, you may build your house.
Do not testify against your neighbor without cause, and do not deceive with your lips.
Do not say, "I will do to him as he has done to me; I will repay the man according to his work."
I went past the field of a {{atsel|slacker}} and by the vineyard of a man lacking judgment.
Thorns had grown up everywhere, thistles had covered the ground, and the stone wall was broken down.
I observed and took it to heart; I looked and received instruction:
A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest,
and poverty will come upon you like a robber, and need like a bandit.

atsel.gloss: Slacker, sluggard, one habitually averse to labor. Verse 30 pictures his field overrun with thorns and his wall broken down, a visible portrait of how small, repeated neglect compounds into visible ruin, a warning Proverbs repeats often, for example 6:6-11, almost word for word.

basic.context: This short supplement, explicitly labeled 'also sayings of the wise,' rounds off the longer collection before it. It insists that courts must convict the guilty rather than flatter them with false acquittal, and it closes with an unforgettable, first-person parable: the teacher walks past a neglected field, sees the ruin, and draws out the lesson that a little more sleep invites poverty like an armed robber.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage mixes direct commands, do not show partiality, do not testify falsely, with an observed parable rather than a command; the teacher does not simply state 'do not be lazy' but shows the ruin and lets the reader draw the conclusion, a method Scripture itself models as effective teaching through observation, not only through direct instruction.
basic.originalLanguages: The atsel, slacker or sluggard, verse 30, is a repeated character type in Proverbs, most memorably here and in 6:6-11, always pictured concretely, a ruined field, a broken wall, never abstractly. The phrase 'a little sleep, a little slumber,' verse 33, uses rhythmic repetition to mimic the slow, almost pleasant slide into ruin that laziness produces.
basic.typology: The call to convict the guilty rather than call them innocent, verse 24, anticipates the perfect justice of Christ's judgment, which never flatters sin but calls it what it is, even while offering mercy to those who repent. The ruined field observed and taken to heart, verses 30-34, anticipates the parables of Jesus, who likewise used observed, everyday pictures, seeds, soils, vineyards, to teach eternal lessons.
basic.christ: The insistence that whoever calls the guilty innocent will be cursed by peoples, verse 24, while blessing comes to those who convict the guilty, verse 25, anticipates the perfect justice of Christ, who never minimizes sin yet offers real mercy to those who confess it, holding justice and grace together at the cross in a way no human judge fully can. And the teacher's method of observed parable, walking past a field and drawing a lesson, verses 30-34, is the same teaching method Jesus later perfects, using ordinary fields and vineyards to reveal the kingdom of God.
basic.god: God's own justice stands behind the courtroom principle of this passage; calling the guilty innocent is a lie about reality itself, which God, the truthful judge, will not bless, while convicting the guilty rightly reflects His own character and draws real blessing. He is also patient enough to let ordinary consequences, a field gone to thorns, a wall fallen down, teach the lesson that direct command alone might not land.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 5:20 echoes the warning against calling evil good and good evil, as in verse 24. Matthew 13 shows Jesus teaching through observed agricultural parables like the one in verses 30-34. Proverbs 6:6-11 repeats the sluggard's ruin almost word for word. Galatians 6:7-9 echoes the certainty of consequence, sowing and reaping, behind the ruined field.
basic.oneStory: This short passage insists on honest justice in the courtroom and lets a ruined, thorn-choked field preach its own sermon about the slow creep of laziness into poverty. Both threads point toward Christ, the perfectly just judge who never calls evil good, and the master teacher who used ordinary fields to reveal eternal truth.

context.literary: A brief, self-contained supplement, 24:23-34, marked by its own introduction, 'these also are sayings of the wise,' 24:23, bridging the longer Sayings of the Wise before it and the Hezekiah collection, chapter 25 to 29, that follows.
context.historical: Ancient Israelite courts depended on local judges and elders at the city gate, making honest testimony and impartial judgment, verses 23-29, a matter of real communal survival, while agricultural neglect, verses 30-34, threatened a family's entire livelihood in a subsistence farming economy.
context.canonical: The warning against calling the guilty innocent, verse 24, echoes throughout the prophets, especially Isaiah 5:20, and the sluggard's ruined field anticipates the New Testament's repeated agricultural parables of sowing, reaping, and harvest.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press honest, impartial judgment as a communal necessity and to teach, through vivid observed example rather than bare command, how small neglect compounds into real ruin.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 23 to 29 are direct prescriptions, do not show partiality, do not testify falsely, do not repay evil for evil, while verses 30 to 34 function as description, an observed scene, from which the reader is invited to draw a personal application rather than receiving a direct command.

debates.question: Does verse 29, 'do not say, I will do to him as he has done to me,' forbid all forms of just legal repayment or restitution?

views.label: It forbids any repayment at all, even lawful restitution
views.case: The verse's blanket language, 'do to him as he has done to me,' could be read as ruling out any tit for tat response whatsoever, including legitimate legal remedy.

views.label: It forbids personal vengeance, not lawful justice or restitution
views.case: The context, following verses on courts and honest testimony, addresses personal vindictiveness rather than the legitimate legal processes Israel's law itself provided, such as proportional restitution, Exodus 21:23-25; the concern is a private grudge dressed up as fairness, not the courts doing their proper work.

debates.bearing: Reading the verse as a warning against personal vengeance, not against lawful courts or restitution, keeps this proverb consistent with the rest of the passage's concern for honest, impartial justice rather than private retaliation.

study.originalLanguages: Atsel, slacker, verse 30, names a recurring character type in Proverbs whose ruin is always shown concretely, a field of thorns, a fallen wall, never merely described in the abstract. The repeated Hebrew phrase behind 'a little sleep, a little slumber,' verse 33, mimics in its rhythm the gentle, almost lulling pace at which laziness slides into poverty.

typology.type: Convicting the guilty rather than calling them innocent, 24:24-25
typology.fulfillment: The perfect justice of Christ, who never calls sin innocent yet offers real mercy to the repentant.

typology.type: The observed parable of the ruined field, 24:30-34
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' own teaching method of drawing eternal lessons from ordinary fields and vineyards, Matthew 13.

study.christ: The courtroom principle that blessing comes to those who convict the guilty while curses fall on those who call the guilty innocent, verses 24-25, anticipates the perfect justice of Christ, who never minimizes sin, yet who alone can also justify the truly guilty who come to Him in repentance and faith, holding justice and mercy together in a way no human court fully can, Romans 3:26. The teacher's method here, observing a ruined field and drawing out its lesson, verses 30-34, is the same method Jesus later perfects in His parables, using ordinary vineyards, seeds, and harvests to reveal the kingdom of God.
study.god: God's own truthful character stands behind the demand for honest judgment; to call the guilty innocent is to lie about reality, which the God of truth will not bless, while rightly convicting the guilty reflects His justice and invites real blessing. He is also the patient teacher who lets ordinary consequences, an untended field slowly overrun with thorns, preach a sermon that direct command alone might not fully land.

crossReferences.note: Woe to those who call evil good and good evil, echoing the warning against calling the guilty innocent in 24:24.
Jesus teaches through observed agricultural parables, the same method used in Proverbs 24:30-34.
Repeats the sluggard's ruin almost word for word, reinforcing the lesson of Proverbs 24:33-34.
A man reaps what he sows, echoing the certain consequence behind the ruined field.

study.oneStory: This short section calls God's people to honest, impartial justice and lets an ordinary ruined field preach the lesson that small, repeated neglect compounds into real poverty. Both threads find their fulfillment in Christ, the perfectly just judge who never calls sin innocent, and the master teacher who used the everyday world to reveal the truths of His kingdom.

quiz.q: What does verse 24 say happens to whoever tells the guilty 'you are innocent'?

options.text: Peoples will curse him, and nations will denounce him.
options.note: False acquittal draws widespread condemnation, not mere private disapproval.

options.text: He is rewarded with rich blessing.
options.note: That blessing belongs instead to those who rightly convict the guilty, verse 25.

options.text: He faces no consequence at all.
options.note: The verse specifically describes serious social consequence.

options.text: He is mildly criticized but ultimately respected.
options.note: The verse's language, cursed and denounced by peoples and nations, is far stronger than mild criticism.

options.text: "I will do to him as he has done to me; I will repay the man according to his work."
options.note: Personal vengeance dressed up as fairness is exactly what this verse forbids.

options.text: "I forgive you completely."
options.note: This is the opposite of what the verse actually forbids.

options.text: "I will help you regardless of what you have done."
options.note: This is not the statement the verse addresses.

options.text: "I will let the courts decide this matter."
options.note: This statement is closer to the verse's implied alternative, lawful process, rather than the forbidden statement itself.

quiz.q: What did the teacher observe when passing the sluggard's field, verse 31?

options.text: Thorns had grown up everywhere, thistles covered the ground, and the stone wall was broken down.
options.note: Visible, compounding neglect marks the field's ruin.

options.text: A thriving vineyard well cared for.
options.note: This is the opposite of the neglect the teacher actually observed.

options.text: An empty field with no crops planted at all.
options.note: The text specifically describes overgrowth and ruin, not mere emptiness.

options.text: A field partially maintained but with a few weeds.
options.note: The description is far more severe, thorns everywhere and a broken wall, than a few weeds.

quiz.q: According to verses 33-34, what does 'a little sleep, a little slumber' lead to?

options.text: Poverty coming like a robber, and need like a bandit.
options.note: Small, repeated laziness compounds into sudden, forceful poverty.

options.text: Restored strength and better productivity.
options.note: This is the opposite outcome described in the verses.

options.text: No real consequence, since rest is always beneficial.
options.note: The text specifically warns of serious consequence, not neutrality.

options.text: A gradual, gentle decline that is easily reversed.
options.note: The imagery of a robber and bandit suggests sudden, forceful loss, not a gentle, easily reversed decline.

quiz.q: What method does the teacher use to convey the lesson of verses 30-34?

options.text: A first-person observed parable, walking past a ruined field and drawing out its lesson.
options.note: The teacher shows rather than merely commands, letting observation teach the point.

options.text: A direct legal command with a stated penalty.
options.note: This is not the form used; it is observation and reflection, not legal command.

options.text: A dialogue between two named characters.
options.note: The passage is first-person narration of observation, not a dialogue.

options.text: A quotation from an ancient king.
options.note: There is no royal quotation here; it is the teacher's own personal observation.

questions: Verses 24-25 warn against calling the guilty innocent for the sake of comfort or approval. Where are you tempted to excuse wrongdoing, in yourself or someone close to you, rather than naming it honestly?
Verse 29 forbids repaying evil for evil under the guise of fairness. Is there someone you are quietly keeping score against, waiting for a chance to even things out?
The teacher observed a ruined field and 'took it to heart,' verse 32. What small area of neglect in your own life, if you are honest, is slowly turning to thorns?
'A little sleep, a little slumber' pictures how gradual small compromises become large ruin, verse 33. What small daily habit of yours needs correcting before it compounds further?
The whole passage rewards honest speech and honest labor. Where is God calling you to greater honesty this week, whether in words, work, or both?

translationNotes.note: 'An honest answer given is like a kiss on the lips' pictures the intimacy and warmth of truthful speech, a striking image for how valuable candor is between people.
'A man lacking judgment' translates a phrase describing someone devoid of sense or heart, chasar leb, the same root used elsewhere in Proverbs for those who lack basic discernment.
These two verses repeat 6:10-11 nearly verbatim, a deliberate echo showing the sluggard's ruin was proverbial enough to be quoted twice in the book.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 24:23-34. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
